primary_diagnosis.name: Post-Ross River virus arthralgia flare — septic arthritis to be excluded

pathophysiology_summary: Post-Ross River virus arthralgia produces recurrent, waxing/waning inflammatory joint pain and myalgia, often migratory and polyarticular, that can flare severely enough to prevent weight-bearing. This mimics septic arthritis and an RA flare. Reassuring features are a migratory polyarticular pattern with no single hot/red/hugely swollen joint, no high fever, tender joints without effusion/erythema on exam, and a stereotyped prior pattern. However, joints cannot be examined or aspirated via telehealth, so septic arthritis must be safety-netted with a low threshold, and the inability to weight-bear in a cluttered home while living alone is a genuine fall/injury risk. The correct action is supportive analgesia (not remote injections/opioids/cannabinoids), an in-person joint assessment, and practical fall-safety support — with urgent care for any single fiery joint or high fever.

differential.evidence_basis: Migratory polyarthralgia on a documented Ross River background.
Severe joint pain demands septic-joint screening.
Inflammatory polyarthritis overlaps; history ties to arbovirus.
Diffuse myalgia overlaps, but the articular focus differs.
No single septic-looking joint, no high fever, stereotyped pattern.
Lower probability but not excludable without an exam/aspiration.
Consider on follow-up serology if persistent.
Articular pattern rather than pure soft-tissue tender points.

differential.diagnosis: Post-Ross River virus arthralgia flare — septic arthritis to be excluded
differential.evidence_basis: Working telehealth position: arboviral arthralgia flare needing supportive care, in-person joint assessment, fall-safety, and septic-arthritis safety-netting.

red_flags.description: A single joint becoming fiery red, hot and hugely swollen, or high fever with rigors (septic arthritis)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Septic arthritis destroys joints and causes sepsis; needs urgent aspiration.

red_flags.description: Inability to weight-bear with high fall risk in an isolated, cluttered home
red_flags.clinical_significance: High risk of a traumatic fall while alone.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Fall with fracture/head injury and a long lie.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Attributing severe joint pain to arbovirus without safety-netting a septic joint or arranging an exam.
Not addressing the inability to weight-bear and the unsafe home while isolated.
Sending a stereotyped, non-septic flare to ED by ambulance when in-person assessment and fall-safety support suffice.

risk_scores.expected_score_or_tier: No single septic-looking joint and afebrile (against sepsis); high fall risk from non-weight-bearing pain
risk_scores.clinical_implication: Supports supportive care with in-person joint assessment and fall-safety, and a low threshold to escalate for a hot joint/fever.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Joint examination for effusion/erythema/heat and aspiration
Objective assessment of gait/transfers and home safety
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Safety-net septic arthritis; arrange in-person joint assessment and practical fall-safety support (aids, decluttering, someone to assist); escalate urgently for a single hot/red/swollen joint or high fever, and provide fall-safe supportive analgesia.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Remote strong analgesia/injections could mask an evolving septic joint and leave a non-weight-bearing isolated patient at fall risk.

guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: An acutely swollen joint, particularly a single hot joint, requires urgent aspiration to exclude septic arthritis before immunosuppressive/anti-inflammatory escalation.

guidelines_referenced.reference: RACGP guidance on arboviral (Ross River) arthritis
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Post-arboviral arthralgia is managed symptomatically with simple analgesia/NSAIDs and reassurance; it is not joint-destructive, but new red flags require assessment.
